Address: 9161 Huntington Av, San Diego CA 92123 United States
Application Name: Zoning-Unpermitted Business (Residential Zone) Zoning-Unpermitted Business (Residential Zone)
Description: This is a refile of CE-0537576, which was closed for budget reasons rather than a finding that no violation exists. We are resubmitting with additional, more recent photographic documentation as previously directed.The property continues to operate as a base for a commercial painting business ("WOW 1 Day Painting"). On a daily and overnight basis, multiple large logo-branded work vans are staged at the residence, typically two in the driveway and one on the street. This pattern has been documented continuously from August 2025 through June 2026.These conditions exceed the allowances of San Diego Municipal Code §141.0308:§141.0308(a)(1): home occupations must be incidental to residential use and not alter residential character. Daily staging of a commercial van fleet does both.§141.0308(a)(3)(A)/(B): exterior evidence of a business is prohibited. Multiple branded vans in public view are exactly such evidence.§141.0308(a)(3)(C): limits business vehicles on the street. Commercial vans are routinely staged at the property.§141.0308(a)(3)(D): a home occupation may not displace required off-street parking. The driveway is used for fleet storage.§141.0308(a)(3)(E): only one non-resident employee is permitted. Additional individuals have been observed arriving and departing in these vehicles.New since the prior filing: One van has had its branded wrap removed while remaining in active commercial use and staged at the property. This appears to be an effort to reduce the visible evidence of the business rather than to cease the activity, and we respectfully ask that it be considered in that light.We request that Code Enforcement investigate and determine whether a Notice of Violation is warranted.

Address: 1501 Island Av, San Diego CA United States
Description: Shift Apartments, 1501 Island Ave, San Diego CA 92101. Resident: Unit 1311 (formerly 1911). Prior case CE-0540573 closed after my forced relocation.1. JUNE 8, 2026 FIRE-SUPPRESSION SPRINKLER DISCHARGE — BUILDING-WIDE ALARM AND EVACUATION.** On June 8, ~7:48–8:20 PM, a large volume of water flooded my balcony at Unit 1311 for ~30 minutes. Security identified the source as a broken fire-suppression sprinkler on the balcony of Unit 1511 (15th floor). The discharge triggered the building fire alarm, locked the elevators and fire doors, and forced an evacuation including families with children and pets. All "11"-stack units below the 15th floor were impacted. Management confirmed in writing that night (8:29 PM "Emergency Fire Alarm Update").This is the 9th major water-intrusion incident I have experienced in three years at Shift, all impacting the "11" stack. The prior 8 affected my former Unit 1911 from late 2024 through January 2026, tied to the 20th-floor pool equipment room and the 21st-floor pool — including a Nov 23, 2025 interior flood (two-week vacate), a bathroom ceiling leak running continuously Dec 15, 2025–Jan 15, 2026, three chlorinated balcony floods in 2025, and a Jan 3, 2026 19th-floor hallway flood. The cumulative impact rendered Unit 1911 uninhabitable and forced my relocation to Unit 1311 on Feb 1, 2026. The recurring pattern across pool plumbing, pool equipment, and now the fire-suppression system itself reflects **dangerous building conditions and ongoing negligence in repairs.** San Diego Fire-Rescue may share jurisdiction over the June 8 event.2. DEFECTIVE TRASH CHUTE INTAKE DOORS — INCOMPLETE "FIRE-SAFETY COMPLIANCE" REPAIRS.** In a June 2, 2026 building-wide notice, management announced "trash chute repairs and fire-safety compliance work" for June 4–6, scoped as "chute intake doors and related fire-safety components." Floor-by-floor inspection before (May 28/30) and after (June 8) confirms fire-safety defects remain: Floor 10 — trash chute intake door slams open, no hydraulic brake (impact hazard); Floor 13 — door non-functional both May 30 and June 8, indicating no repair attempt; Floor 16 — recycling chute intake door hydraulics non-functional, identical hazard. Under the California Fire Code and Building Code, chute intake doors must be self-closing, fire-rated, controlled-action assemblies. By management's own framing this is "fire-safety compliance" work; the remaining defects are unresolved fire-safety code violations.3. RECURRING TOWER ELEVATOR OUTAGES DESPITE ANNUAL CERTIFICATION — 31-DAY ELEVATOR 3 OUTAGE.** Despite Yearly Elevator Safety Testing on Feb 13, 2026, the three tower elevators had at least four documented unplanned service events between May 2 and June 8: a May 2 building-wide outage from alarm-testing that triggered a false alarm; a sustained Elevator 3 outage from the evening of May 8 not formally restored until June 8 — approximately **31 days**; a June 5 management notice confirming **only one of three tower elevators was operational** that day; and the building-wide elevator lockout from the June 8 sprinkler event. In a 20+ story tower serving elderly and mobility-impaired residents, sustained loss of multiple elevators creates substantial habitability and life-safety risk under Cal. H&S Code §17920.3. The pattern since the February certification indicates deferred maintenance requiring municipal inspection.**REQUESTED ACTION:** Formal inspection of (a) the fire-suppression system, especially balcony sprinkler heads in the "11" stack at/above the 15th floor; (b) the pool, pool equipment room, and 20th/21st-floor plumbing tied to the water-intrusion pattern; (c) every trash chute intake door for self-closing / controlled-action / fire-rating compliance; (d) the three tower elevators including maintenance records and Cal/OSHA certifications since Feb 2026. Coordination with San Diego Fire-Rescue requested for the June 8 sprinkler event.

Description: Neighbor purchased the home over a year ago and since then the construction of different projects have been non stop. There is constant humming of an air compressor, grinding, banging, jackhammering, every power tool possible has been heard. The residents, and employees screaming, dogs squealing. While constructing the metal fence, (currently happening) the air compressor has been going since 8am into the evening. Yesterday they turned it off at 7:45pm. Right now it is 5:45pm and we can’t enjoy our meal and can’t tune it out with the tv.  In addition to the construction machinery, they also play loud music. The loud speaker’s base is so loud that it rumbles inside our home. We can’t come home from a very long day of work and relax because we’re forced to hear the base, music, and tools when we just want to come home to peace. This has been ongoing for over a year. They don’t seem to take any consideration for their neighbors with the noise, the parties, and the dogs. I beg that you do something because it’s causing us a lot stress and health issues for us. We appreciate them taking the effort and money to improve their home, it’s great, but it’s been over a year and it’s non stop, and also getting worse. I must also mention, they also worked on the fence this Sunday and Memorial Day, a holiday. Again, we understand some noise, but the noise in the evenings and Sunday when we NEED to rest is simply disrespectful. Based on the trucks that he has parked outside, he is a construction/contractor business man, he should know the rules. I must also add that he uses a leaf blower every single day, multiple times a day to blow dust into the middle of the street , sides, and even the blows stuff into the storm water. I hate complaining about my neighbors but this is excessive. To maintain peace, I humbly ask that you please do not disclose any identifying information to them. I am not asking for them to come to a hault, I’m simply asking that they be considerate with the noise. Don’t blast the music and construction at the same time and be considerate of the time. We want to enjoy our home in peace when not working. I’m sure the surrounding neighbors do as well.

Description: There is a dog at 4962 Cape May Ave, Unit H that cries out in distress for hours on end, every day, day and night. The dog owner (named Paige) and the rental agency have been spoken to, made aware of the problem, and have been offered solutions, but have continued to fail to address the issue. I personally offered to buy the owner bark deterrent devices, and gave her several practical tips on how the issue could be addressed. The dog owner has stated that she is gone most of the day and night at work and school and that there's "nothing she can do". The unit's direct neighbor, Danaelle Rodriguez, has submitted hundreds of complaints to their rental agency over the past year, yet the problem continues. Danaelle also submitted an Investigation Request to the city (CE-0541184), yet her request was closed without any resolution to the problem.Most nights, this dog yelps out in distress until past midnight. Their windows are left open, and there are at least 30 San Diego residents living within earshot of their window. You can likely hear this bark from the next street over, it's that loud. I live 10 feet from their window and I have lost hours of sleep from this disturbance each time it happens. Description: Installed a side yard solid fence (not street side) along a property line and driveway extending to public sidewalk.  The purpose of the fence is to block the view of the  adjacent property's driveway (1321).   At sidewalk, the fence is 3' high, but as the fence extends from the sidewalk along the driveway, it steps up to 4' after running 4'.  Then after a further 4' (total of 8' from sidewalk) it steps up to 5'.  Then after another 4' (total of 12' from sidewalk), it steps up to 6'.  At this point, the fence is >10' forward of the front of the house.  It is my understanding that, due to obvious safety concerns associated with a driveway view corridor, a solid fence is to be no higher than 3' measured from the sidewalk and extending back 10'.  This fence far exceeds that - and this is a heavily trafficked area, being located one block from a school and two churches.  It was my further understanding that side yard fences extending from the front of a house to the public right-of-way (the sidewalk) were not to exceed 3'.  I am no expert on this, but this fence clearly introduces a significant visual impairment when backing out of the driveway that seems to cause an unnecessary public safety hazard.

Description: I am submitting this on behalf of my 82-year-old mother, XXXX, at her request, who currently lives at XXXX. next door to the house located at XXX Whittier St. where the work is being done.  The houses on Whittier St. are on a hillside and are stepped with each having a retaining wall that supports the yards and houses that are above.  The house at 3028 Whittier St is where the hill grade transitions from gradual to significant.  Our concern is that work has been undertaken in the backyard at 3028 Whittier St over the last month including the removal of a substantial amount of dirt with a new retaining wall being constructed creating multiple tiered levels on the east side of the yard near where the existing retaining wall for XXXs located.  The new retaining wall being constructed at 3028 supports fill dirt for an already existing pool and required a large amount of excavation. This work is in addition to other work done in the yard.  After speaking to the owner of 3028 Whittier St (XXXXX) on Wednesday, May 20th he was evasive when asked if he had received or talked to the City of San Diego regarding permitting of the project and we were not able to locate any records on the City’s Open DSD. Over six months ago XXXXX approached XXXXX stating that he wanted to undertake work in his backyard including replacing a common fence located between XXXX Whittier St. that was on top of the existing retaining wall at XXXX Whittier St.  At that time my brother, XXXX XXXXX, and XXXX met with Mr. XXXX at his request and they understood it was his right to improve his property but any work that could impact the retaining wall or that was substantial he should consult with the City to ascertain  if he needed permits and to hire a licensed contractor to ensure that any work was built to code given that each of the houses’ retaining walls on the street are essential to holding up the houses above and a worse case failure could  start a domino effect leading to houses possibly sliding down the hillside into Nimitz Blvd.  At that time Mr.XXX  stated that he was going to put the project on hold and research permitting. On Tuesday, May 19th, after work had been going on at his house for weeks, Mr. XXX contacted XXXX stating that he wanted to meet to discuss the fence project again.  Both XXX and XXX met with Mr. XXX on Wednesday, May 20th, and at that time XXX and XXX were able to see the amount of work that had already been done and the proximity to XXXXX existing retaining wall.  Later in the evening of Wednesday May 20th, I XXXX, talked to Mr. XXX to clarify if he received permits for the work.  He stated that he was an engineer (I clarified that he was not a civil engineer or licensed in the state of California), that he had done his own research, and discussed with his contractor and they both concluded that they did not need permits. I asked if he had discussed with the City of San Diego to check that their conclusions regarding permits was correct and he said he did not.  In multiple conversations with Mr. XXX dating back months, we discussed that having the correct permits pulled and the City inspect the work, if required, was in his best interest for safety and future liability if his work caused a failure. He seemed to understand the consequences.At this point my mother XXXX is worried about the impacts to her retaining wall and the hillside. She is currently undergoing cancer treatment, and this has had an impact on her mental health.  Your urgent review of this matter, especially when there is a Super El Nino year predicted, is appreciated. It is important to note that work is continuing at his house.  Thank you!

Address: 2720 Broadway, San Diego CA
Description: Multiple units have experienced years of recurring mold issues and water retention/damage (#6, #16, #17 #21, #23, amongst others). Landlord has been notified by at least 6 different tenants over the past few years. Landlord demonstrates a repeated pattern of blowing off tenants' concerns and quietly providing superficial, incomplete repairs on a unit-by-unit basis rather than comprehensively addressing the underlying structural defects that cause these issues to recur across the entire complex. Multiple tenants are continually forced to purchase dry bags and other supplies out of pocket in an attempt to fight mold growth, poor structural ventilation, and likely water damage within the walls. There is an ongoing, extensive black mold infestation across the complex, with at least one unit's issues (#17) progressing to the point of affecting the habitability of the apartment. Tenant of #17 had to relocate for multiple weeks (1/2026 - 2/2026) due to significant health issues caused by these living conditions. Landlord was notified of these health issues alongside medical documentation, but denied temporary relocation assistance to the tenant. Further, third party inspection of unit #17 showed high levels of moisture within this north exterior wall, detected to be 65% humidity in bedroom and 45% moisture intrusion in walls. To date, landlord has only performed minimal superficial mold remediation in this unit (carpet cleaning, removal of two areas of drywall in bedroom along North and East exterior walls), but the mold issue persists in this and other units. Landlord insists that these mold and water damage issues are resolved and were actually never an issue of habitability in the first place, despite repeated complaints from multiple tenants and the tenant of #17 suffering ongoing health effects from mold exposure. Further, in addition to the above mold and water intrusion issues, Unit #6 previously suffered extensive water damage due to water leaking from the adjacent laundry room. When tenants initially raised concern to landlord, they repeatedly resisted performing the necessary repairs and remediation efforts. Only after being pestered by the tenants for weeks did they provide portable dehumidifier units and make other superficial repairs to flooring etc. Even after these repairs, the laundry room showed ongoing mold and water damage at the baseboards. The Landlord has since replaced the baseboards, despite not resolving the underlying issues within the walls. This neglect eventually resulted in the tenants of unit #6 ending their lease. Upon moving out, these tenants found further mold infestation in their north facing wall. Now that the unit is vacant, Landlord has quietly added a window to the unit's previously windowless bathroom, but continues to deny a broader structural problem. Exterior walls on the North and South sides of the complex are also full of large bubbles in the plaster, suspected due to water damage, with moss even growing along the North wall. On a weekly basis, there are streams of water running off of the corners of the roof onto the walkways below, even on days when it hasn't rained for weeks. Roof shows signs of water intrusion/damage and mold in these areas as well.

Application Name: Building-Uninhabitable Living Conditions Building-Uninhabitable Living Conditions
Description: I am requesting a code enforcement inspection at 5430 Baja Drive, San Diego, CA 92115, due to serious building code and habitability violations affecting my bedroom.The sole window in my bedroom has been permanently altered by the property owner. The lower half of the window opening has been filled with a window-mounted air conditioning unit (Midea brand), and the upper half has been filled with a painted OSB/plywood board that has been caulked and sealed directly into the window frame. The window cannot be opened in any way. There is zero operable window area in this room.VIOLATIONS I AM REPORTING:1. EMERGENCY EGRESS — The bedroom has no operable emergency escape or rescue opening, in violation of California Residential Building Code § R310. This is a fire and life safety hazard.2. INOPERABLE WINDOW / NO VENTILATION — The window is permanently blocked and cannot provide any natural ventilation, in violation of California Health & Safety Code § 17920.3(a)(7) and (a)(8), which require proper ventilating equipment and minimum amounts of natural light and ventilation.3. ZERO OPERABLE WINDOW AREA — California law requires at least 4 square feet of operable window area in a habitable room. This bedroom has none.4. UNPERMITTED ALTERATION — The property owner has permanently modified the window opening by installing a plywood/OSB panel caulked and sealed into the structural frame. This does not appear to have been permitted.5. WOOD ROT / STRUCTURAL DETERIORATION — Visible wood rot and frame deterioration are present where the plywood meets the window frame, in violation of California Civil Code § 1941.1 and Health & Safety Code § 17920.3.I have photographic evidence of all conditions described above and am available to provide access for an inspection at any time. Please contact me at xxxxxMonthly rent for this room is $1,313. I have been living in these conditions since July 23,2025.
